title: Alcohol ink marbling on water
materials: isopropyl alcohol 91% (for thinning and creating spread effects)
yupo paper, glossy card stock, or other non-absorbent surface (standard alum-mordanted paper does not work for alcohol inks)
sources: SYNTHESISED — alcohol ink marbling is a modern craft technique with no pre-1928 PD source. Draws on the chemistry of alcohol inks (solvent-based transparent dyes, repelled by water surface tension) and general marbling surface-tension principles from Halfer (1893) — public domain.
notes: Alcohol inks are solvent-based dyes that repel each other and spread dramatically on a water surface. Very different from carrageenan marbling: no size needed, no mordanting, non-absorbent substrate required. Covers: using a shallow water-filled tray, dropping alcohol inks on the surface (they spread in concentric rings), adding a drop of isopropyl alcohol to push pigments outward, stirring with a stylus, laying a piece of yupo paper or glossy card on the surface and lifting. Strong colours with loose organic shapes. Ventilation required (alcohol vapour). Rapid technique — a sheet takes under 2 minutes. Compare with suminagashi (ink-on-water, brush-blow) and carrageenan acrylic marbling.
